speeches.speaker: Mrs. Hardie
speeches.title: ANNUAL HOLIDAY BILL. (Hansard, 12 November 1937)
speeches.llm_rationale: The speech is about a Holidays with Pay Bill for manual workers, arguing for workers' rights to paid holidays. While women workers are briefly mentioned as also deserving paid holidays, this is in the context of labor rights and social welfare, not women's political rights or representation.

speeches.speaker: Sir EDWARD PARROTT
speeches.title: Mr. MONTAGU'S STATEMENT. (Hansard, 6 August 1918)
speeches.llm_rationale: The TARGET speech is entirely about Indian self-government, constitutional reform, and education policy in India. The brief mention of women's enfranchisement is a passing comparative reference ('just as it has revolutionised our views with regard to the enfranchisement of women in this country') and does not constitute substantive argument about women's political rights.

triggers.context: ... just as it has revolutionised our views with regard to the enfranchisement of women in this country. The War has made us a grateful debtor to I...

speeches.speaker: Mr. SWIFT MacNEILL
speeches.title: PARLIAMENT AND LOCAL ELECTIONS BILL (Hansard, 17 April 1917)
speeches.llm_rationale: The speech is primarily about the unconstitutional prolongation of Parliament's life, Irish Home Rule, and the abuse of wartime powers. The single brief mention of women's suffrage is only a passing reference to Asquith's changed opinion on the subject, not an argument for or against women's political rights.

triggers.context: ...n with reference to his change of opinion on the subject of women's suffrage, between himself and some Greek poet whose name I forget. B...

speeches.speaker: Mr. Kaufman
speeches.title: NEW SYSTEM OF ELECTORAL REGISTRATION (Hansard, 15 December 1999)
speeches.llm_rationale: The TARGET speech is entirely about the overseas vote and the Representation of the People Act, arguing against allowing tax exiles and long-term emigrants to vote in UK elections. It does not address women's political rights, representation, suffrage, or any related topic.

speeches.speaker: Viscount Morpeth
speeches.title: REGISTRATION OF VOTERS (IRELAND) —ADJOURNED DEBATE. (Hansard, 26 March 1840)
speeches.llm_rationale: This speech is entirely about Irish voter registration reform and the elective franchise for (male) Irish tenants and leaseholders, with no mention of women's political rights, suffrage, or representation.

speeches.speaker: Sir B. CHADWICK
speeches.title: TRADE DISPUTES AND TRADE UNIONS (AMENDMENT) BILL. (Hansard, 28 January 1931)
speeches.llm_rationale: The TARGET speech is about the Trade Disputes and Trade Unions Bill, focusing on picketing, trade union political levies, and the 1926 General Strike. While it briefly mentions 'Conservative working men and women' as constituents, it does not address women's political rights, representation, voting rights, or related topics.

speeches.speaker: Mr. McKENNA
speeches.title: Case of Mr. Ball (Insanity). (Hansard, 22 February 1912)
speeches.llm_rationale: The TARGET speech is about a prisoner named Mr. Ball who became insane, with the medical officer attributing it to dwelling on 'Votes for Women' and 'political prisoners'. This is not about women's political rights or representation but rather a medical/prison matter where suffrage is only incidentally mentioned.

triggers.context: ...ver a strong one, dwelling continually on the questions of "Votes for Women" and "political prisoners." I have not yet obtained any inf...

speeches.speaker: Mr. Lamb
speeches.title: INDEMNITY BILL. (Hansard, 11 March 1818)
speeches.llm_rationale: This speech by Mr. Canning is entirely focused on the Indemnity Bill, the suspension of the Habeas Corpus Act, government spies and informers, and parliamentary reform debates. The brief passing mention of women being excluded from universal suffrage proposals is a rhetorical aside mocking Sir F. Burdett, not a substantive argument about women's political rights.

speeches.speaker: Mr. Smith
speeches.title: UNEMPLOYMENT. (Hansard, 22 November 1939)
speeches.llm_rationale: The speech is primarily about unemployment policy, the Nazi approach to unemployment, and the Labour movement's resistance to cuts in social services. While women are mentioned incidentally (e.g., Miss Wilkinson as a representative, young women forced into labor camps), the speech is not about women's political rights or representation.

speeches.speaker: Mr. Richard Shepherd
speeches.title: Government Information (Hansard, 10 December 1996)
speeches.llm_rationale: The speech is entirely about freedom of information and open government, with no reference to women's political rights, suffrage, representation, or participation in political life.

triggers.context: ...embers of the House, although we are all duly elected on an equal franchise. However, the information was available to Members if they ...

speeches.speaker: Mr. Frank Allaun
speeches.title: PUBLIC EXPENDITURE (Hansard, 9 March 1979)
speeches.llm_rationale: The speech is entirely about economic policy, housing conditions, public expenditure, and employment — it does not address women's political rights, representation, voting, or participation in political life.

speeches.speaker: MR. GREGORY
speeches.title: PAPERS MOVED FOR (Hansard, 29 May 1863)
speeches.llm_rationale: The speech is entirely about British foreign policy toward the Ottoman Empire, Turkey, Serbia, and Eastern Europe, with no mention of women's political rights or representation.

speeches.speaker: MR. T. P. O'CONNOR
speeches.title: EDUCATION BILL. (Hansard, 12 May 1896)
speeches.llm_rationale: This speech is entirely about an education bill concerning voluntary/Catholic/Protestant schools, school boards, and religious education in Britain and Ireland. There is no discussion of women's political rights, suffrage, representation, or related topics.

speeches.speaker: MR. P. A. TAYLOR
speeches.title: OPENING OF NATIONAL MUSEUMS, &c. ON SUNDAYS.—RESOLUTION. (Hansard, 8 June 1877)
speeches.llm_rationale: This speech is entirely about opening national museums and galleries on Sundays for public recreation and instruction. It does not address women's political rights, voting, representation, or any related topic.

speeches.speaker: Mr. Kevin McNamara
speeches.title: NORTHERN IRELAND (APPROPRIATION ORDER) (Hansard, 12 June 1972)
speeches.llm_rationale: The speech is entirely about Northern Ireland politics, internment, prisoners, industrial development, and economic policy. There is no discussion of women's political rights or representation.

speeches.speaker: Mr. Muff
speeches.title: PROLONGATION OF PARLIAMENT BILL (Hansard, 26 October 1943)
speeches.llm_rationale: The speech is about the prolongation of Parliament's life and the conduct of elections, including concern that troops abroad cannot vote. While it briefly mentions 'men and women' in the armed forces, it does not address women's political rights or representation as a distinct topic.

speeches.title: DEBATE ON THE ADDRESS (Hansard, 2 November 1962)
speeches.llm_rationale: The speech is primarily about espionage, counter-intelligence, and security vulnerabilities in British diplomatic missions, particularly regarding the Vassall case. While women are briefly mentioned as targets of Russian surveillance, this is in a security/espionage context, not related to women's political rights or representation.

triggers.context: ...weakest point of our diplomatic representation in Moscow, and I believe that the same goes for our diplomatic missions in other countries behind the Iron Curtain. The women are not immune. I know...

speeches.speaker: Mr. HAMAR GREENWOOD
speeches.title: PAY, ETC., OF THE ARMY (VOTE 1). (Hansard, 5 June 1913)
speeches.llm_rationale: The TARGET speech concerns military matters including the treatment of soldiers, war widows and children's pensions, and Army equipment deficiencies. While widows and children are mentioned as deserving better pensions, this is not framed in terms of women's political rights or representation.

speeches.speaker: Mr. BANFIELD
speeches.title: AMENDMENT OF LAW. (Hansard, 18 April 1934)
speeches.llm_rationale: The speech is entirely focused on budget policy, unemployment insurance cuts, taxation, export trade, and economic conditions. There is no discussion of women's political rights or representation.

speeches.speaker: Mr. Kirkwood
speeches.title: MILITARY TRAINING BILL. (Hansard, 4 May 1939)
speeches.llm_rationale: The speech is about conscription and the engineering union's opposition to it, not about women's political rights or representation. The brief mention of women voting in Birmingham is about a ballot on conscription, not about women's suffrage or political representation.

triggers.context: ...a secret ballot, and the women voted seven to one for conscription....

speeches.speaker: Mr. T. SHAW
speeches.title: CLAUSE 2.—(Rates of contributions in case of young men and young women.) (Hansard, 22 November 1927)
speeches.llm_rationale: The speech is primarily about unemployment insurance contributions and benefits for workers aged 18-21, critiquing the financial and social adequacy of the scheme. While women are occasionally mentioned alongside men as workers affected by the clause, the speech is not about women's political rights or representation.

speeches.speaker: Sir R. CRADDOCK
speeches.title: FIRST SCHEDULE.—(Composition of Provincial Legislatures.) (Hansard, 10 May 1935)
speeches.llm_rationale: The TARGET speech is about the communal award for Bengal, the Poona Pact, and the distribution of seats between Hindus, Mohammedans, and depressed classes in India. The brief mention of 'two women's seats on the general list' is incidental and not the focus of the speech, which is not about women's political rights or representation.

speeches.title: CLASS IV. (Hansard, 17 June 1897)
speeches.llm_rationale: The TARGET speech is entirely about education policy, specifically the half-time school system, teacher retirement, and class sizes. There is no discussion of women's political rights or representation.

speeches.llm_rationale: The speech is about motor car allocation for transporting voters to polling stations in rural constituencies, not about women's political rights or representation.
